industries.name: Single-Crystal Nickel Superalloys
uses: Rhenium at 3–6% in single-crystal nickel superalloys (CMSX-4: 3%Re, CMSX-10: 6%Re, René N6: 5.4%Re) dramatically improves creep resistance at temperatures above 1000°C — Re partitions to the gamma matrix and slows dislocation climb, enabling higher turbine inlet temperatures and greater engine efficiency

Each GE GE90, GE9X, Pratt & Whitney PW4000, and Rolls-Royce Trent engine turbine blade contains rhenium — advanced turbofan engines use 2nd and 3rd generation single-crystal alloys with 3–6% Re in the highest-temperature stages

Rhenium allows turbine blades to operate 50–100°C hotter than Re-free alloys — each 10°C increase in turbine inlet temperature improves thermal efficiency by ~0.5%, justifying rhenium's very high price (~$1,500–3,000/kg)

Military jet engines: F119 (F-22), F135 (F-35), and other advanced fighter engines use higher Re-content alloys for maximum performance — military engines push closer to material limits than commercial engines
Turbine blade recycling recovers rhenium: end-of-life aircraft engine blades are remelted and rhenium is recovered — secondary rhenium from superalloy recycling provides ~20% of supply

companies.name: GE Aerospace
companies.use: Major rhenium consumer in single-crystal turbine blade casting. Its GE9X (777X) uses CMSX-10 (6%Re) 3rd generation single-crystal blades in the highest-temperature turbine stages. One of the world's largest individual rhenium end-users.

companies.name: Pratt & Whitney (RTX)
companies.use: Major rhenium consumer in its GTF (geared turbofan) engines for A320neo and A220. Uses René N6 and other Re-containing single-crystal alloys in HPT blades. Major rhenium demand driver alongside GE and Rolls-Royce.

companies.name: Freeport-McMoRan
companies.use: Major rhenium producer — rhenium is recovered from molybdenite roasting at copper-molybdenum mines. Its Henderson molybdenum mine and Climax mine are significant rhenium sources. Chile (CODELCO, Molymet) dominates world rhenium supply at ~50%.

industries.name: Petroleum Reforming Catalysts
uses: Platinum-rhenium (Pt-Re/Al₂O₃) bimetallic catalyst for catalytic reforming of naphtha to high-octane gasoline and aromatic petrochemicals (benzene, toluene, xylene) — Re dramatically reduces catalyst coking and extends catalyst cycle life between regenerations

Pt-Re reforming catalysts operate at 490–540°C and 10–35 bar hydrogen pressure — the Re allows lower H₂ partial pressure operation (semi-regenerative and continuous reforming) improving reformate yield and octane
Rhenium catalysts for selective reduction reactions in fine chemical and pharmaceutical synthesis — Re₂O₇/Al₂O₃ for metathesis and selective oxidation reactions

companies.name: UOP (Honeywell)
companies.use: World's largest licensor of catalytic reforming technology (CCR Platforming process). Supplies Pt-Re/Al₂O₃ reforming catalysts to oil refineries globally for producing high-octane gasoline blendstock and aromatic petrochemical feedstocks.

companies.name: Axens (IFP Energies Nouvelles)
companies.use: Major reforming technology and catalyst supplier. Produces Pt-Re reforming catalysts for its Octanizing and Dualforming continuous catalytic reforming processes licensed to refineries worldwide.

industries.name: Specialty Alloys & Electronics
uses: Tungsten-rhenium (W-Re) thermocouple wire: W5%Re/W26%Re thermocouple (Type C) for temperature measurement to 2320°C in inert and reducing atmospheres — the highest-temperature thermocouple in common use for furnace monitoring and aerospace testing
W-Re alloys for X-ray tube rotating anodes: rhenium addition to tungsten improves ductility and thermal shock resistance of X-ray tube anodes, reducing cracking under repeated thermal cycling

Rhenium filaments in mass spectrometer ion sources and scanning electron microscope (SEM) electron guns — rhenium wire emits electrons efficiently and withstands oxidizing conditions better than tungsten
Rhenium-molybdenum and rhenium-niobium superconducting alloys for specialized cryogenic applications

companies.name: Omega Engineering (Spectris)
companies.use: Major thermocouple and temperature sensor manufacturer. Produces W/Re Type C and Type D thermocouples for ultra-high-temperature measurement in aerospace, nuclear, and semiconductor furnace applications.
